claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to understand a bit about your child (or children).

**First, let me ask: Do you have a child, and if so, how old are they?**

The age of your child is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me:
1. How many children you're inquiring about
2. Their age(s)
3. Their name(s) (this helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm inquiring about my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me work through Alex's eligibility.

Since Alex is 8 years old, he falls into the **under 16** category. Now I need to check a few more things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, **is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

At 8 years old, I expect he's not working at all, but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment. This is a standard check to ensure children aren't in full-time paid employment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all. He's only 8, so he's still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question: **Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

In other words, is Alex receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name? 

Given that he's 8 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is that he's not receiving these benefits, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 8-year-old!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue checking the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check: **Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Again, since he's only 8 years old and in primary school, I expect he's not in an apprenticeship, but this is part of the standard eligibility criteria I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's definitely not in an apprenticeship. He's far too young for that - he's just in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status. **Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family (and NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm here in England with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm: **Are you responsible for Alex?**

You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you've already mentioned you do!)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care - including things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

Based on what you've told me, you live with Alex at home, so this requirement is met. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. Alex lives at home with me, and I take care of all his needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to ask: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- A grandparent
- Another relative or guardian

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me and Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check Alex's living situation. **Which of the following best describes where Alex is currently living?**

- **In hospital** (receiving medical care as an inpatient)
- **In care** (looked after by the local authority)
- **Fostered** (living with foster parents)
- **Normal living** (living at home with you)

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for a few months receiving treatment, but I still regularly buy things for him - you know, his favorite snacks, magazines, clothes, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand - that must be a difficult time for you both. Let me check the rules about children in hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to understand Alex's hospital stay better. **How long has Alex been in hospital cumulatively?**

Here's what I need to know:
- Has he been in hospital for **more than 12 weeks** in total?
- If he's had multiple hospital stays, were they separated by less than 28 days? (If stays are less than 28 days apart, they count together towards the 12-week limit)

You mentioned he's been there for a few months. Could you give me a more specific timeframe? For example:
- About how many weeks or months has he been in hospital?
- Has this been one continuous stay, or has he been home and then readmitted?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for about 15 weeks now - it's been one continuous stay this time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. 15 weeks is a significant stay, and I hope Alex is doing well. Let me continue checking the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is important! You mentioned that you regularly buy things for Alex - his favorite snacks, magazines, clothes, and that sort of thing. 

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?**

The good news is that even though Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, you can still be eligible for Child Benefit if you're regularly spending money on him. This includes things like:
- Snacks and treats
- Magazines or books
- Clothes
- Other items for his care or comfort

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like you ARE regularly spending money on Alex. Can you confirm this is the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I regularly spend money on him - I visit and bring him his favorite snacks, buy him magazines to keep him entertained, get him new clothes when he needs them, and anything else he needs or wants while he's there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's wonderful - I'm sure those visits and items mean a lot to Alex during his hospital stay. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Just one final question: **Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's care or accommodation costs while he's in hospital? 

Generally, if the local council is paying for a child's accommodation or maintenance, you cannot get Child Benefit. But if they're not paying, or if the hospital costs are being covered by the NHS (which is typical for medical treatment), then you should be fine.

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance. It's just regular NHS hospital treatment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me record the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You Are ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit!

**For your son Alex (age 8):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**

### Why you qualify:

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements:
- ✅ Alex is under 16 years old
- ✅ Alex is not in paid work
- ✅ Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right
- ✅ You normally live in the UK
- ✅ You are responsible for Alex (you live together and provide for his needs)
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✅ **Important:** Even though Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (which exceeds the standard 12-week limit), you still qualify because you regularly spend money on him while he's in hospital - buying snacks, magazines, clothes, and other items. This is a key exception to the 12-week hospital rule.
- ✅ The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge
5. **Payments:** Child Benefit is typically paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers well, and that this Child Benefit support helps during this challenging time. Is there anything else you'd like to know about the claim process?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 and qualifies automatically for the age requirement; (2) He is not working; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK; (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together and provide for his needs; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex while he's in hospital (snacks, magazines, clothes, etc.), which is an exception to the 12-week rule; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - he's receiving regular NHS treatment.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex's extended hospital stay of 15 weeks, the claimant maintains eligibility because they regularly spend money on Alex during his hospitalization, which is a key exception to the standard 12-week hospital stay limit. All other eligibility criteria are met.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 and qualifies automatically for the age requirement; (2) He is not working; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK; (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together and provide for his needs; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex while he's in hospital (snacks, magazines, clothes, etc.), which is an exception to the 12-week rule; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - he's receiving regular NHS treatment.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex's extended hospital stay of 15 weeks, the claimant maintains eligibility because they regularly spend money on Alex during his hospitalization, which is a key exception to the standard 12-week hospital stay limit. All other eligibility criteria are met.
